text.title: django-quality-security Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules covering transport security headers, rate limiting, input validation, exception discipline, and secret management. Each rule has a canonical source.

rule.statement: Production Django MUST set DEBUG=False, SECURE_SSL_REDIRECT=True, SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE=True, CSRF_COOKIE_SECURE=True, SECURE_BROWSER_XSS_FILTER=True, SECURE_CONTENT_TYPE_NOSNIFF=True, X_FRAME_OPTIONS="DENY", SECURE_HSTS_INCLUDE_SUBDOMAINS=True, SECURE_HSTS_PRELOAD=True. SECURE_HSTS_SECONDS MUST be ramped 300 → 3600 → 31536000 across multiple deploys, never set straight to 31536000 on a new domain. Configure CSP via Django 6.0+ ContentSecurityPolicyMiddleware (or django-csp) starting in report-only mode. CI MUST run `python manage.py check --deploy --fail-level WARNING` and block merges on any WARNING / ERROR.
rule.rationale: Each setting closes one specific attack class. HSTS is a one-way ratchet: a misconfigured deploy with the full year value locks out users for a year. CSP report-only first surfaces violations without breaking the page; switching to enforce after a few days of telemetry avoids the 24h post-deploy fire drill.

rule.statement: Authentication endpoints (login, password reset, registration, token refresh) MUST be throttled at ≤ 5 attempts/minute per IP. Public API endpoints MUST have per-user AND per-IP throttle classes. Use DRF `DEFAULT_THROTTLE_CLASSES` for DRF projects OR `django-ratelimit` for non-DRF views — never both on the same endpoint. Install django-axes for account lockout (username-based) and configure `AXES_IPWARE_PROXY_COUNT` to match the actual proxy chain.

rationale.source: OWASP Authentication Cheat Sheet + django-axes docs https://django-axes.readthedocs.io/

rule.rationale: A 5/min cap blocks 7200 brute-force attempts/day vs unlimited. Per-IP alone is bypassable by IP rotation; account lockout adds the orthogonal username dimension. Stacking django-ratelimit and DRF throttles double-counts traffic — users hit only one limit until 2N attempts, defeating the cap.

rule.statement: All user input MUST traverse a Django Form or DRF Serializer before being used in ORM queries, file operations, or business logic. Forbidden: raw SQL with string concatenation of user data, `.objects.extra(...)` with user input, mass-assignment via `Model(**request.POST)`. File uploads MUST validate magic-byte content (not just extension) and size cap. Service functions MUST call `instance.full_clean()` before `save()` so model-level validators and clean() methods fire.

rationale.source: OWASP Django Cheat Sheet https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Django_Security_Cheat_Sheet.html + DRF serializers docs

rule.rationale: The ORM parameter-binds queries safely only when called as documented; .extra and string SQL re-open SQL injection. Form/serializer schemas reject unexpected fields (mass-assignment defence). Extension-only file checks miss polyglot uploads (`.jpg` carrying PHP).

rule.statement: Production Django code MUST NOT use bare `except:` or `except Exception:` as the only handler. Catch specific exceptions (Model.DoesNotExist, ValidationError, requests.Timeout, IntegrityError). Every `logger.error(...)` on a caught exception MUST include `exc_info=True` so the traceback ships to Sentry / logs. Never swallow without re-raise.
rule.rationale: Bare except hides AttributeError / KeyError / NameError that indicate programming bugs and should fail loudly. Without exc_info, Sentry receives a message and no traceback — incident triage takes 10x longer.

rule.statement: SECRET_KEY, database credentials, API keys, payment tokens MUST load from environment variables via `os.environ` or `django-environ`. Never commit secrets to git, never log password / token / credit_card / PII. Register a Django system check that emits `Error` when `SECRET_KEY` equals or starts with `django-insecure-`. Sentry MUST use `before_send` to scrub PII keys from event payloads (see django-quality-logging r2).

rationale.source: OWASP secret management + Django checks framework

rule.rationale: Secrets committed to git history are effectively permanent; rotation is forced. The default `django-insecure-*` key is the most common Django security misconfiguration — a startup-time check costs zero and catches it before exposure.
